data.title: U.S. Dollar Up, Gold/SPY Down
data.description:
Whenever any asset has made an extended move in one direction, and is about to make a huge move in the opposite direction, the vast majority of analysts, brokerages, and investors become convinced that the trend of recent years will continue indefinitely. One clear example in 2026 is the very popular belief that the U.S. dollar is going to collapse and that it will no longer serve as the world's reserve currency. This perception is so pervasive that one often finds financial articles that begin with a phrase such as "because the U.S. dollar is likely to continue to decline in upcoming years," followed by the conclusion that a weakening U.S. dollar will lead to higher prices for most other assets, including precious metals and global equities.

I holler for the U.S. dollar (while almost everyone else is expecting a greenback collapse)

The myth of an upcoming accelerated pullback for the U.S. dollar has encouraged at least three well-known financial publications to highlight the alleged upcoming plunge for the greenback. Barron's, The Economist, and Forbes each featured a separate cover story about how the U.S. dollar will collapse. At least the cover artists had original visual presentations, as you will see from these three magazine covers, beginning with Barron's from February 23, 2026:

Barron's large-font feature headline states, "The reign of the [U.S.] dollar is coming to an end. What investors can do about it." The first line of the article proclaims, "The [U.S.] dollar is in decline, and investors have to learn to live with it."

Earlier that month, on February 5, 2026, The Economist reached the same conclusion, illustrated creatively with a green snake:

The accompanying Economist headline declares, "The age of a treacherous, falling [U.S.] dollar," with the story beginning, "Those holding American assets will have to get used to it."

On February 17, 2026, Forbes featured a burning U.S. dollar bill on their magazine cover:

Forbes' headline warns, "IMF Issues Serious U.S. Dollar Collapse Warning" with this statement: "High debt, combined with persistent inflation and high interest rates, puts massive pressure on the U.S. dollar and risks a long-term erosion of its dominance as a global reserve currency."

As I was writing this essay, Elon Musk - timely as always - issued a warning that the U.S. dollar would plunge while Bitcoin surges in price. I plan to write several paragraphs or more about cryptocurrencies and how they will become nearly worthless over the next few years, but I want to remain focused here on the repeated "urgent" and "must act now" tone of most of the media's coverage of the greenback and its future prospects.

The U.S. dollar has been in a powerful bull market since March 2008 when it had slumped to its all-time bottom, followed by a slightly higher bottom in July 2008. Near the July 2008 low, the greenback was so unpopular that supermodel Gisele Bündchen declared that she wanted to be paid going forward only in euros, which that year had reached an all-time high just above 1.60 U.S. dollars. I expect the U.S. dollar to climb substantially higher during the collapse of the AI bubble, just as it had done during the collapse of the internet bubble. The U.S. dollar index will very likely reach its highest level since 1985 by the end of the decade, while I expect the euro to fall below 90 U.S. cents within a few years or less.

There were no recent cover stories, or hardly any other feature articles, in any well-known media publication pointing out that the U.S. dollar index has repeatedly rebounded from all selloffs. By itself, repeated recoveries from sharp short-term pullbacks are how most powerful bull markets begin. Unfortunately, they aren't recognized until after the most important part of the gains have already occurred.

As the U.S. dollar at first gradually and later more convincingly moves higher rather than lower over the next few years, this will lead to eventual depressed valuations for most assets, including stocks, commodities, corporate bonds, and cryptocurrencies. Residential real estate will lose perhaps half of its current value in many neighborhoods. Only U.S. Treasuries, TIPS, and related U.S. government debt is likely to benefit from a strengthening U.S. currency that will encourage investors to put more of their money in U.S. dollar-denominated time deposits.

Far too many analysts anticipate "permanently" high energy prices, just as in the summer of 2008 prior to historic losses by the end of 2008

Besides the nearly unanimous negativity surrounding the U.S. dollar, the media have featured numerous other myths which are in sharp contrast to the way that the most experienced investors in any given sector have been behaving. One popular recent fairy tale is that because of Iran or some other combination of factors, energy prices will allegedly remain "permanently high." If this sounds familiar, it was also heard frequently during 2008, especially in the summer. Top corporate insiders sold energy shares more aggressively during the past several weeks than they had done at any time in recent decades, including 2008, and shortly afterward, funds of energy shares - including XLE - began what will eventually become dramatic percentage declines.

Energy prices soared to all-time zeniths during the first half of 2008, and then when nearly all analysts had turned bullish, we had one of the biggest percentage declines for gasoline and related commodities during the second half of 2008. I expect an approximate repeat during the next several months or so.

Everyone is saying gold 6000, almost no one is forecasting 4000 (or lower)

Gold and silver had their turn at reaching "permanent rally" status not many weeks ago when we had both aggressive selling by top executives of gold and silver mining companies in addition to commercials (the equivalent of insiders for futures trading) featuring substantially above-average short-to-long ratios for gold, silver, and platinum. Those ratios remain lopsidedly in favor of lower, rather than higher, prices. The same media outlets, analysts, and brokerages which warned investors not to buy precious metals when they were especially depressed at other key buying points, including January 2016, March 2020, and the autumn of 2022, have tooted among the most bullish horns in recent weeks. Top corporate executives of precious metals mining companies had done some of their strongest-ever insider selling when gold and silver had been peaking several weeks ago.

My favorite misleading media excuse for gold to rise in price is central bank buying. Throughout their history, central banks have been repeatedly and aggressively buying assets near peaks and selling them near bottoms. The U.K. central bank sold most of its gold in the late 1990s, when prices were at all-time lows in real terms. The central bank of Canada sold most of its gold near the end of 2015 and the start of 2016, when gold prices were similarly very depressed and were set for substantial upcoming gains. China has been selling U.S. Treasuries and buying gold recently, thereby both buying high and selling low. The next few years will demonstrate why they should have been doing the exact opposite.

Extreme overallocation to any asset, in this case large-cap U.S. stocks, inevitably leads to major losses

Let's turn to the U.S. stock market, where the average U.S. household allocation to equities in recent decades has averaged very close to 26%. In March 2000, the total allocation to stocks briefly touched 51.1% of total household assets - by far an all-time record until that point. In March 2026, this allocation climbed to 55.1% - as you can see from the following chart from last month courtesy of Mark Hulbert, who has persistently done excellent research on this topic:

In addition to the highest-ever allocation to the stock market, more than double the typical amount for the first time in history, those who are most knowledgeable about their companies' future prospects have done their highest-ever selling relative to buying:

According to Bloomberg, the current AI bubble has become more overpriced relative to earnings, not only compared with typical valuations but when measured against the internet bubble of 1999-2000:

John Hussman has a useful benchmark which allows you to compare any year since 1928 with the present, with clear evidence of investors recently substantially overpaying for U.S. stocks:

A combination of 1) ordinary investors having by far their highest allocation to the stock market in history; 2) close to the 99th percentile for valuations, including price-to-sales, price-to-book, and price-to-GDP; along with 3) top corporate insiders making their heaviest-ever sales in history both in absolute U.S. dollars and also as a ratio to total insider buying, provide compelling evidence for an impending dramatic percentage decline especially for those shares which have experienced the most intense selling by top corporate executives, including the CEO. Of course, this doesn't tell us the shape of the bear market, or how high a particular index will climb before it collapses, or when the bear market will end, or how often it will bounce along the way. There is no way to know how high an overpriced asset will climb before it dramatically retreats, or when it will occur. The same is true with anything near a historic bottom: it is unknowable how low it will continue to fall before it bottoms, or when that will happen. However, you can be certain that all assets - especially those which have been priced the farthest away from fair value - will eventually regress to the mean and usually beyond to an approximately opposite extreme.

From the intraday high on March 10, 2000 to the intraday low on October 10, 2002, QQQ lost 83.6% of its value, assuming that you reinvested all dividends. Since most valuation, allocation, and insider activity measures have approached all-time records relative to fair value in 2026 compared with their greatest extremes during the internet bubble of 1999-2000, it is mathematically probable that the total percentage loss for QQQ over the next few years will be greater than the internet bubble's plunge rather than lesser. Investors are not aware how likely it is that they will lose 5 out of 6 dollars by remaining in these shares, and will likely hang in there almost all the way to the bottom when they finally sell out of fear of prices dropping even lower.

Disclosure of current holdings:

Below is my current asset allocation as of 4:00 p.m. on Monday, April 20, 2026. Each position is listed as its percentage of my total liquid net worth. The long positions should add up to about 100%, while the short positions all use U.S. Treasury bills as collateral.

I recently sold most of the stocks I had bought at all depressed points since early 2025 as I had done in late 2021 and whenever the likelihood of a significant pullback was greatest, while adding to PSQ. I also bought more TLT, as this massively shorted fund has continued to steadily form higher lows for 2-1/2 years while paying 4 dollars in annualized dividends.

The order is as follows: 1) U.S. government bonds; 2) shorts; 3) bear funds; 4) precious metals; 5) individual U.S.-listed stocks.

data.title: Sohu: The Market Is Ignoring The Cash And Mispricing Changyou
data.description:
Introduction

Sohu.com Limited (SOHU) remains, in my view, an undervalued stock because the market still looks at it as a complex bundle of assets: one weak Sohu/media segment, one profitable Changyou gaming segment, and a balance sheet that is much stronger than the stock price suggests.

If we look carefully at its balance sheet, the company, as of year-end 2025, had $128.3 million of cash and cash equivalents and $702.4 million of short-term investments, against only $358.0 million of total liabilities. Even after taking a very conservative view of the restricted cash mobility inside the structure, the company still appears to have a meaningful margin of safety relative to its current equity value.

This thesis does not need aggressive divestitures, break-ups, or a specific tender structure to work. Rather, it comes from being very practical—recognizing that the company still owns a profitable gaming franchise, that the weak Sohu (media) segment is already heavily discounted by the market, and that the balance sheet remains strong—the excess cash can be used for shareholder-friendly steps to increase the share price.

Therefore, I am highly bullish on this stock.

Company Background

Sohu is a company that many investors now mostly associate with legacy issues: old internet-media assets, a VIE structure, and a history of portfolio complexity. But the current company is already much simpler than it appears at first glance. Sohu today is really a combination of two parts: the Sohu business, which consists mainly of marketing services and other Sohu services (delivered through the Sohu News App, Sohu Video App, m.sohu.com, and www.sohu.com), and Changyou, whose business consists of online games and the platform-channel business centered on 17173.com.

This matters because while many investors might think that this company needs more corporate restructuring, in my view, the investability already comes from current fundamentals. The corporate structure can be simplified over time, and there may be a case for monetizing media assets, but this thesis argues first that the stock is undervalued today even without depending on a breakup.

Where Sohu Stands Today

As of the 2025 annual report, the company owns one meaningful operating franchise and some other assets. The meaningful franchise is Changyou. In 2025, Changyou generated $508.9 million of revenue and $236.8 million of operating profit. The gaming side is still dominated by the TLBB franchise. The company states that TLBB PC generated $306.9 million of revenue in 2025, and Legacy TLBB Mobile generated $41.4 million. These two parts together remain central to the revenue of the group.

The Sohu segment, in 2025, generated only $75.4 million of revenue and posted an operating loss of $330.6 million. The segment looks unprofitable relative to what we saw in Changyou. So, when I step back, I do not see one mediocre company; rather, I see one profitable gaming platform effectively funding one structurally weak media bucket. Apart from this, Sohu also has a large liquid balance sheet that the market still discounts heavily because of structure, geography, and governance.

The Company Update of Recent Performance

So, the company has a profitable Changyou segment, a weak Sohu/media part, and a strong balance sheet with significant cash. Moreover, the company has been actively repurchasing stock. As of February 20, 2026, Sohu had repurchased 8.21 million ADSs under the current repurchase program for the cost of about $107 million. The board had already increased the authorization to $150 million and extended it to November 10, 2026. That is an important signal. It tells us management is at least willing to use buybacks.

At the same time, the current results need to be normalized to understand them properly. Consolidated operating loss in 2025 was $93.8 million, while reported net income was $394.1 million (because of a very large $443.6 million income tax benefit). That means headline earnings are not the right metric for valuation. Furthermore, the company reported net cash used in operating activities of $4.8 million in 2025, so this is not a clean free-cash-flow compounding stock. Rather, it has to be viewed first as a balance sheet and capital allocation story. That difference is very critical to understanding this thesis properly.

Why The Stock Trades At A Discount

There are several reasons for this:

First, the company's corporate structure is heterogeneous in nature. Media, other Sohu services, online games, and platform-channel assets do not belong to one clean category, and that is the first reason for the discount.

Second, the Sohu segment is generating relatively little revenue compared with Changyou. In my view, the market does not trust that the media side can earn acceptable returns, and looking at the 2025 segment numbers, that doubt is reasonable—and hence the second reason.

Third, the balance sheet structure matters. The company had $245.9 million of restricted net assets at year-end 2025 due to Chinese mainland dividend and reserve rules. This means the market cannot simply treat every dollar of cash on the balance sheet as free cash that the company can simply distribute to shareholders, and therefore, the third reason.

Fourth, there is also a control (float) issue. As of February 20, 2026, Charles Zhang beneficially owned 44.45% of the company, with Photon Group holding 42.97%. That means public float is not very large, and thus, capital-allocation flexibility is not unlimited.

Investment Thesis

The thesis is simple. The market is not properly giving credit for the fact that cash plus short-term investments alone stood at $830.7 million at year-end 2025. If we subtract $358.0 million of total liabilities, that leaves about $472.7 million before further constraints. Then, even after subtracting the disclosed $245.9 million of restricted net assets and leaving aside a practical $10 million capex and operating reserve, we still get about $216.8 million of conservative deployable cash.

Against a current equity value of roughly $385 million, this indicates that investors are assigning only modest value to the operating business after a very conservative liquidity haircut. But the operating business still includes Changyou, which generated $236.8 million of operating profit in 2025. That disconnect is the reason I think the activist case is valid. The stock does not need a Dutch tender or a breakup to work. It only requires the market to stop valuing the whole company as if the profitable gaming business deserves little credit. If Changyou remains stable, if the Sohu/media drag does not worsen materially, and if management continues using capital more responsibly, the discount can narrow meaningfully from current levels.

Why The Stock Can Work From Here

In my view, the value-unlock path is more of a gradual rerating than a single event.

I am not assuming any media divestiture in this thesis, although there could be a case for it over time. The base case is simpler. Changyou remains profitable; the weak Sohu/media segment does not deteriorate further in a way that overwhelms the rest of the company, and the market begins to give more credit to the balance sheet and less penalty to the structure. That alone can help narrow the discount.

The bull case is that management continues repurchasing shares under the already authorized program, becomes more disciplined around the weak Sohu segment, and gives investors clearer capital-allocation messaging. If that happens, the market may stop valuing Sohu as one frustrating bundle and start giving more appropriate credit to the profitable gaming business and the company's liquidity.

Catalysts & Risks

The biggest risk here is execution—if management continues to buy back stock only gradually, the discount can persist longer than investors want. The second risk is concentration in its operations. The filing of the company says clearly that TLBB PC and Legacy TLBB Mobile remain the main revenue generators. If those assets weaken faster than expected, the thesis becomes weak. The third risk is the usual PRC's ability to deploy its cash and bring change to the capital structure. Restricted net assets are real, and so is friction from remittances.

That said, there are also clear catalysts—a larger tender, continued share repurchases, tighter discipline around the Sohu segment, and any other shareholder return accretive steps like dividends—all will lead to higher share price appreciation.

Conclusion

Sohu owns one profitable gaming franchise and one loss-making legacy segment inside a single structure (which the market does not like)—and this is precisely why the stock is interesting.

The company does not need an aggressive turnaround or a specific tender structure for the shares to work. It requires the market to recognize that, even on a conservative framework—using only cash and short-term investments, deducting liabilities, considering the restricted-net-asset constraints, and leaving a capex reserve—the stock still appears to be assigning too little value to Changyou and too much weight to the weak Sohu segment. So, my activist case is straightforward: the opportunity is investable today on current fundamentals, and any improvement in capital allocation, transparency, or discipline could help the market close the discount more meaningfully from here.

data.title: VIOV: This Small Cap Value Fund Is Worth A Look
data.description:
VIOV Overview

The Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Val Index Fund ETF (VIOV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in small-cap American-listed value stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming value stocks within the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities. While initially a laggard post Liberation Day, VIOV has outperformed peer small-cap ETFs — including VBR and VIOG — over the past six months, and I think it has a solid set-up going into the back half of 2026. I have recommended investors consider the Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF (VBR) or Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth Index Fund ETF (VIOG) as passively managed, low-fee options, but VIOV has barged its way into the conversation with recent outperformance and a key differentiator that I think matters more in this environment: a built-in profitability screen that filters out the junk.

After further review, my analysis shows that VIOV is a decent investment vs. other passively managed peers. The fund has rallied over the past 6 months, and has shot back up the past two weeks - though all eyes remain on a war resolution. VIOV's benchmark target is the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index, and the underlying benchmark generally includes U.S. small-capitalization companies. VIOV is in line for a strong 2026 with its current industry weights, and following Morgan Stanley's call for a bullish 2026 in financials, I like the fund has a good way to play small caps - making me rate it a cautious buy.

Fund Breakdown

VIOV was launched on September 7, 2010, by Vanguard with a mandate to track the performance “that measures the investment return of small-capitalization value stocks in the United States.” The fund employs an indexing investment approach designed to track the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index — and overall, it’s done a solid job following those trends over the long haul. The fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of ~$1.7Bn and a daily volume of ~48,000 shares traded worth over $5MM a day. The idea of the fund isn’t bad, and the portfolio turnover is around 47%, which is a bit higher than VIOO’s given the additional rebalancing required for the value methodology as stocks migrate in and out of the value segment.

Upon further review of the ETF’s results, I like that the fund has had a strong recent stretch against peers. The bottom line for these funds is that within the small-cap universe, you need to be more selective — there are simply a lot of poorly run companies that can drag down the full index. The fund employs a 0.10% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager, slightly higher than VIOO’s 0.07%, but still extremely cheap in absolute terms and more than worth it given the profitability filter discussed below. Ultimately, the fund is solid, and I think it’s set up to outperform based on its recent results, history, and ongoing macro tailwinds for value-oriented small caps.

A key selling point for VIOV that I don’t think gets enough credit is the profitability screen embedded in its parent index. The S&P SmallCap 600 requires companies to have positive as-reported earnings over the most recent quarter and the most recent four quarters combined before being admitted to the index. This earnings requirement has been a meaningful contributor to the S&P 600’s consistent historical outperformance over the Russell 2000 — delivering roughly 1.6% higher average annual returns with lower volatility since 1994, with quality factor exposure explaining about 74.7% of that outperformance. When you layer a value tilt on top of that quality filter, you get a portfolio that’s weeding out both overvalued names and loss-making businesses simultaneously. In an environment where small caps have seen significant dispersion in profitability, this matters.

The fund employs a 0.10% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager, similar to VIOG and VBR, which makes sense given the lack of portfolio customization. Ultimately, the fund is solid, and I think it's set up for an outperformer based on its previous results, history, rate-cutting cycle, and ongoing refunding of President Trump's tariffs, which will ease the burden for small caps that can't easily access lots of liquidity.

2026 Outlook

While volatility may remain high in 2026, by cutting through the noise, I think 2026 will be a decent year for stocks, and I especially think the catch-up small-cap trade will be intact. Big tech companies like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) recently reported CapEx numbers in the hundreds of billions, which spooked investors given the lack of clarity on a profitability plan for AI spend, but this spend should flow through the economy and be a boon for small business (if energy prices can get back to reasonable levels).

I liked Morgan Stanley's (MS) recent overview; led by Mike Wilson, Morgan Stanley sees 2026 as "early days" of a new bull market cycle, when the recovery broadens out from strong but narrow pockets to the broader economy. Financials, industrials, and healthcare are all on his target screens as high-quality, better-priced alternatives to some of the AI froth, and VIOO is well positioned in these sectors. The Fed will likely cut rates one or two more times this year with Kevin Warsh likely to be confirmed, which also bodes well for small-cap growth.

Previous Results and Fund Portfolio

When comparing VIOV’s portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the breakdown worth flagging. As of Mar. 31, 2026, the portfolio captures the small-cap value universe with a higher financials weighting versus VIOG and a higher healthcare weighting compared with VBR — two sectors I expect to continue performing well into 2026. With a trailing PE of ~17-18, you’re getting small-cap exposure without paying an AI-driven multiple, and that valuation gap versus the broader market is meaningful. And VIOV’s index methodology captures not just the cheapest third of the S&P 600 by style, but also admits some blend names from the middle third based on their style traits — so the portfolio is broader than a traditional deep value screen. Almost 30% of the portfolio overlaps with the S&P SmallCap 600 Growth Index. This breadth is a feature, not a bug — it means VIOV isn’t so entrenched in the value camp that it misses the better growth stories within the small-cap space.

Risk Profile

From a risk perspective, a beta of 1.22 notes that the fund will have more volatility than the benchmark — and that has borne out in past results with lower lows and some strong bounce-backs. Additionally, when evaluating VIOO's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, VIOO's Sharpe ratio for the fund is 0.34, well below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns and in the negatives, indicating some caution is warranted. From a macroeconomic perspective, small-cap growth stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. With the Federal Reserve recently cutting rates and given VIOV's decent performance vs. peers, I think it's a fine option and one to look at.

Additionally, oil prices continue to remain between $90-100 for WTI and this will be a struggle for small businesses. The hope that some tariff refunds and fuel stabilization with the end of the war is a potential boost, but its worth flagging this risk.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that the last several months have redeemed many of my worries. From a macro perspective, there's a good tailwind for small caps, and the fund is well positioned on financials and healthcare. That said, there are risks and the fund's historical Sharpe ratio and oil prices are worth flagging.The fund is a pure play basket of small caps and is very liquid. While there are risks of small caps being hit by new tariffs and having more volatility than the broader index, I think VIOV is a solid option if investors want exposure in this space and I rate it a cautious buy.

data.title: Repay Holdings: Attractive Valuation Amid M&A Activity
data.description:
Introduction

Shares of Repay Holdings (RPAY) have recovered from early 2026 weakness and are now up roughly 10% year-to-date, principally driven by a $4.80/share acquisition proposal by RPAY's largest shareholder, Forager Capital. A broad rebound in software shares has also contributed to the reversal in RPAY stock.

In this article, I will go over the proposal details, discuss RPAY's valuation, and highlight the company's medium term growth potential, principally driven by the KUBRA acquisition. I rate RPAY a Buy, with my positive outlook underpinned by:

  • A 13.3% trailing equity free cash flow yield achieved with a modest 46% net debt in the capital structure.
  • Significant 2026-2028 free cash flow growth potential, driven by revenue momentum in the near term and acquisition synergies in the medium term.
  • Sizable upside to the $4.80/share acquisition offer, providing a potential near-term catalyst if the deal is ultimately closed.

Proposal Details

RPAY released the following news release regarding the takeover offer:

Repay Holdings Corporation (NASDAQ: RPAY) (“REPAY” or the “Company”), a leading provider of integrated payment processing solutions, today confirmed receipt of an unsolicited, non‑binding proposal from Forager Capital Management, LLC (“Forager Capital”), a stockholder of the Company, to acquire the outstanding shares of the Company for $4.80 per share in cash.

REPAY’s Board of Directors (the “Board”), consistent with its fiduciary duties, will review the proposal in consultation with the Company’s financial and legal advisors. Stockholders are not required to take any action at this time.

J.P. Morgan Securities LLC is serving as financial advisor, and Troutman Pepper Locke LLP and Sullivan & Cromwell LLP are serving as legal counsel to REPAY.

For me, the key takeaway is that the offer is non-binding, implying that there is no firm commitment from Forager Capital to execute the offer. Coupled with the intent of RPAY's board to review the offer, there is no certainty that a deal will ultimately be reached. In essence, RPAY's board may decide that the offer is too low, advising shareholders not to accept it.

We also see that Forager Capital has not bid up the shares' market price to the takeover proposal level, indicating that $4.80/share is a price it is willing to pay only if it gains control of RPAY. Here it is important to note that the offer is all-cash. In light of significant equity price volatility in recent weeks, it is perhaps understandable that Forager Capital is taking a more cautious approach toward acquiring additional shares on the open market. In essence, spending too much on the RPAY acquisition (which may ultimately fail to materialize) could lead to Forager Capital missing out on other opportunities in the equity markets.

All in all, given the high uncertainty characterizing both the offer itself, as well as current equity market conditions, investors need to consider both the company's current market valuation, as well as its valuation at the proposed $4.80/share takeover price, before making a decision whether to sell RPAY stock or hold it for longer. This is precisely what I will do in the next section.

Valuation

As I write this, RPAY trades around $4.05/share, giving the company a market capitalization of roughly $368 million (factoring in a 90.86 million diluted share count). The company's net debt position stands at around $311 million, resulting in an enterprise value of $679 million.

With $49.1 million in 2025 free cash flow, RPAY's equity free cash flow yield of around 13.3% is quite attractive, notwithstanding the circa 46% weight of debt in the company's capital structure. What is more, the company targets adjusted EBITDA of $136.5-141.5 million in 2026 (before the effects of the KUBRA acquisition which I will discuss shortly), potentially resulting in 2026 free cash flow marginally above $60 million. As such, we see that at the current valuation RPAY not only offers an attractive free cash flow yield, but also comes with significant double-digit free cash flow growth.

At the proposed $4.80/share acquisition price, RPAY's free cash flow yield remains attractive at approximately 11.3%.

In conclusion, whether the Forager Capital acquisition closes or not, I think RPAY's current valuation is quite cheap even if the upside to $4.80/share is ultimately not realized in the short term.

KUBRA Acquisition

Amidst the Forager Capital takeover offer, RPAY is in the process of acquiring the Canadian bill payments and customer communications company KUBRA, with closing expected in Q2 2026. While transaction related expenses may weigh on RPAY's 2026 financials (hence indicating downside risks to the adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow outlook issued before the KUBRA deal announcement), RPAY ultimately expects the deal to boost free cash flow by roughly 25% in 2028, with total synergies seen at about $20 million.

While the transaction will increase RPAY's leverage profile in the short term, the company plans to pay down debt in H2 2026 and into 2027, ultimately resulting in a reasonable net leverage ratio (calculated as net debt divided by adjusted EBITDA) of below 3x in 2028. For reference, the net leverage ratio would have been roughly 2.25x based on the 2026 outlook before the KUBRA acquisition.

To sum up, I think RPAY has a meaningful standalone growth strategy even if the Forager Capital deal ultimately fails to materialize, providing another incentive for long-term investors to consider RPAY at its current valuation. As such, taking into account RPAY's current valuation, potential near-term upside to the Forager Capital $4.80/share takeover offer, as well as free cash flow growth from the KUBRA acquisition in the medium term, I believe that a Buy rating is appropriate for RPAY.

Risks

In the near term, significant uncertainty remains whether RPAY's board embraces the Forager Capital takeover. Indeed I think it is quite likely they will strive to improve the offer above $4.80/share, with the ultimate result dependent on broader equity market sentiment. If the rebound in software stocks continues and the war in Iran ends in a negotiated settlement, I think odds are Forager Capital will improve the offer. Alternatively, if software valuations slump and oil prices surge amid a protracted Middle East war, the deal for RPAY may fail to materialize even at $4.80/share, as the offer is non-binding.

Higher leverage and execution risks will likely characterize H2 2026 and 2027, as RPAY absorbs the effects of the KUBRA acquisition. While the deal may ultimately bring meaningful upside for investors with a 2028 horizon, sector headwinds in the payments industry (stemming from increased competition from AI) could lead to a persistently low valuation of RPAY, while a high debt burden will arguably prevent the company from executing meaningful share repurchases.

Conclusion

RPAY stock has surged in recent days, boosted by the Forager Capital takeover offer, as well as a shift in sentiment toward software companies. Despite the strong gains, I believe RPAY remains attractively valued, ranking it a Buy.

My bullish outlook is underpinned by near-term potential gains to $4.80/share, as well as the company's sizable 2028 free cash flow growth potential, assuming synergies from the KUBRA acquisition are realized in full.

The non-binding nature of the Forager Capital takeover presents a key risk in the near term, while intense sector competition and disruption from AI may lead to RPAY underperforming its 2028 financial targets.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: The Abel Arbitrage And Stronger Returns Post-Buffett (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I upgrade Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) (BRK.A) stock’s rating to a Strong Buy as the market mispriced/ignored its structural evolution. I am observing that under CEO Greg Abel, Berkshire is shifting from a founder-led compounding investment into a hyper-efficient capital return and operational optimization business. My thesis is also based on the catalyst that is the instrumentalization of Berkshire’s $373.3 billion cash fortress. On this, the main indication for me is Abel’s immediate resumption of stock repurchases along with his personal $15.3 million open-market acquisition.

This is a main monitorable mentioned in my last Berkshire stock coverage.

“Looking forward, the important catalyst to watch for is the first major capital allocation decision by Abel. A value-accretive acquisition and the reinstatement of a big and consistent stock repurchase program will encourage me to reassess the new leadership’s capability to deploy Berkshire's massive cash to scale Berkshire stock’s value.”

This move (stock buyback) forms a floor at the $465-$454.05 ($465 is the technical support level as expected in the last coverage, and now $454.05 is the updated technical support). Moreover, BNSF may extract massive long-term FCF through the SMART-TD labor arbitrage and the ¥272.3 billion Tokio Marine debt-equity spread trade that points to aggressive ROE synthetics. The main risks to this thesis are the $50 billion tail risk from Berkshire Hathaway Energy’s [BHE] Oregon wildfire litigation (that is risking parent-level contagion) and GEICO’s protracted telematics lag (that is risking highly adverse selection against algorithmic competitors).

My last thesis rated Berkshire Hathaway a ‘Hold’ based on the argument that its legacy value-oriented portfolio is out of line with today’s tech-tilted market. Although core segments offer stability, Berkshire’s dependence on mature physical economies lags dynamic and self-cleansing indices like the S&P 500/SPY and Nasdaq-100/QQQ. In that article, I observed that Berkshire’s growth is halted by the law of large numbers capping its trillion-dollar asset base and a huge idle cash pile exerting heavy opportunity cost. At the time, the looming leadership shift risks the erosion of the historical Buffett premium. As a result, I advised patient opportunism, that is, to avoid new positions until market corrections provide better/logical entry points and the successor, Greg Abel, moves capital into value-accretive acquisitions to reboot growth. As expected, here we are.

What Can Take Berkshire Hathaway's Stock Price Higher?

After 56 years, Berkshire Hathaway is no longer headed by Warren Buffett (as CEO). Post January 1, 2026, Greg Abel holds the mind of the $1.02 trillion mCap system. The shift has happened with a complex inflection that is Q4-FY2025 operating earnings dropped ~30% YoY to $10.2 billion (based on a 54% drop in insurance underwriting), and the huge cash stake is at $373.3 billion. I am focusing on 4 forward factors that will build Berkshire’s stock price uptrend over the next decade. To begin with, the first upside factor is Abel's aggressive capital return policy of Berkshire’s $373.3 billion in cash and short-term U.S. Treasury reserves. Under Buffett, the buyback policy was capped by a strict, subjective, intrinsic-value assessment. This old policy led to zero buybacks in Q4. In line with my expectations, Abel has immediately shifted the policy. In March 2026, Berkshire resumed stock repurchases. Along with that, Abel personally indicated Berkshire’s stock's high undervaluation by buying $15.3 million in Berkshire stock (through the Gregory E. Abel Revocable Trust) in early 2026. At a 1.34x price-to-book with $373.3 billion of liquidity (~37% of the mCap), Abel holds the capability to work out one of the largest corporate stock retirements. To understand the magnitude, if Abel utilizes ~$100 billion (for example) of this dry powder for buybacks at current valuations, he can retire ~10% of the outstanding float. This move can drive up a long-term double-digit accretion to EPS, operational cash flow per share, and tangible book value [TBV] per share. This can happen regardless of macroeconomic stagnation, in my opinion.

Alongside this, the Japanese insurance/trading house yield advantage is the second factor. Berkshire's initial investments in the 5 Japanese trading houses yield 10% on the $24.5 billion cost basis and are now valued at $194 billion in equity total. My focus here is on how Abel is expanding this advantage. To explain more here, in early FY2026, Berkshire used its high credit rating for the issuance of ¥272.3 billion (~$1.7 billion) in ¥-denominated bonds across 6 tranches that were priced at a 90-bps spread (3.084% coupon). Importantly, these funds were applied to build a 2.5% (scaling up to 9.9%) holding stake in Tokio Marine. Logically, by borrowing in yen at ~3% to buy Japanese stocks attached to higher yields and stock buybacks, Berkshire is sharply forming a large FX-hedged spread trade. More in this direction, National Indemnity’s deal with Tokio Marine opens up a new pipeline for reinsurance synergies. On a forward basis, these reinsurance synergies can merge and expand with Berkshire's $176 billion float and its Japanese retail distribution capabilities.

Then, the third bullish catalyst is BNSF's post-SMART-TD margin advantage with anti-PSR stabilization. BNSF is lagging Union Pacific Corporation (UNP) (its main competitor) in operating ratio due to its resistance to applying draconian precision scheduled railroading [PSR] cuts. In Q4-FY2024, BNSF has expensed a $290 million charge for a major deal with the SMART-TD labor union. This deal supports BNSF in redeploying brakepersons to conductors and engineers. This move’s advantage is that it is restructuring BNSF's labor cost curve. To answer how? By FY2025’s end, BNSF’s operating margin expanded to 34.5% with a 65.5% operating ratio. Moreover, in the 2026 CEO letter, Abel pointed to a hard focus on BNSF lead Katie Farmer, saying that he would be “disappointed if we do not deliver a substantial improvement over the next few years.” More here, Abel marked that every 100-bps scale-up progress in operating margin can bring in ~$230 million in incremental cash inflow from ops. Long story short, through the SMART-TD labor flexibility + Abel’s utility-operator background, BNSF can scale up $1 billion+ in annual FCF over the mid-term without hurting/modifying network reliability (based on ~5% point operating margin improvement).

With all that, the fourth catalyst is Berkshire’s role as the lender of last resort in a possible P&C hardening scenario. In FY2025, GEICO restored margins at a 72.3% loss ratio, which is down from 81% in FY2023. These margins derived $6.8 billion in underwriting earnings (pre-tax). But BHRG (Reinsurance) has counteracted price declines under an influx of alternative capital (in the catastrophe market). On this side, Abel and Ajit Jain (VC, Insurance Ops) are reducing premium growth. Here, Berkshire’s Insurance Ops are moving away from inadequately priced risk to preserve capital. In this, my forward thesis argues based on catastrophic convexity. To clarify more, if a systemic shock (like a massive cyber event/unprecedented climate disaster) wipes out undercapitalized alternative reinsurance markets, Berkshire will become the giant with the strong balance sheet to step in as the lender-of-last-resort. More on how? Berkshire is capable of deploying tens of billions in capacity overnight at super-premium pricing. This super-premium pricing can capture multi-decade ROEs that no competitor can match at scale, in my opinion.

What Can Take Berkshire Hathaway's Stock Price Lower?

On the list of downsides, the first catalyst in effect is the BHE Wildfire Regulatory Compact collapse and liquidity drain. To clarify more, BHE is countering a big risk from strict liability and inverse condemnation laws regarding wildfires in the Western U.S. Although PacifiCorp recently agreed to a $575 million federal settlement with the DOJ (Feb 2026), this is a fraction of the larger danger. The James mass complaints in Oregon involve ~1,700 plaintiffs seeking $5 million in economic, $25 million in non-economic, and massive punitive damages (a 0.25x multiplier). As Oregon law permits the doubling of economic damages, these claims currently seek ~$50 billion. To appeal the Multnomah Court verdicts, PacifiCorp is needed to post supersedeas bonds. It has already posted $606 million for just 109 plaintiffs. With Case Management Order No. 11 scheduling dozens of trials in 2026 and over 100 in 2027/2028, BHE faces a possible liquidity crisis. If the Oregon Supreme Court denies the stay, BHE may be unable to obtain the necessary surety bonds to stay payment of damages during appeals. Abel warned that BHE will not invest growth capital (like for AI hyperscaler data centers) if the regulatory compact is broken. If BHE is forced into bankruptcy to ring-fence liabilities, it may permanently impair Berkshire’s consolidated earnings power and wipe out the utility-growth premium embedded in Berkshire stock.

Moreover, GEICO’s Adverse Selection Telematics is also problematic. Although GEICO attained a strong 87.1% combined ratio in FY2025, it was done at the expense of market share. GEICO's policies in force continue to decline as it pushes aggressive rate increases. Competitors [like The Progressive Corporation (PGR)] that possess a 5- to 10-year structural lead in telematics and algorithmic pricing are now reducing rates to capture the safest drivers. GEICO counters a classic adverse selection death spiral. To add more on this, as safer drivers leave for cheaper telematics-based policies at Progressive, GEICO is left with a riskier pool that is forcing further rate hikes and further policy attrition. If GEICO cannot progressively implement its telematics overhaul by 2027, it can permanently lose its status as the low-cost auto insurer. In my opinion, that will degrade the base source of Berkshire's cost-free float.

Apart from that, I want to point out the HomeServices Antitrust Contagion factor. HomeServices of America settled the Burnett antitrust litigation for $250 million (to be paid over 4 years) regarding the inflation of real estate commissions through the Cooperative Compensation Rule. The $250 million settlement does not shield Berkshire from secondary contagion. On this issue, U.S. District Judge Stephen Bough ruled that Berkshire's brokerage unit must face a new proposed antitrust suit and rejected the single enterprise defense. Furthermore, HomeServices is facing Texas state law deceptive trade practices claims totaling $9 billion. Beyond the direct legal liabilities, the structural change to the National Association of Realtors [NAR] commission rules permanently impairs the high-margin, high-volume cash flow system of the traditional real estate brokerage model. This is effectively neutering the long-term terminal value of HomeServices.

Beyond all that, I am also considering the Law of Large Numbers and Dead Equity drag. As of Q4-FY2025, Berkshire’s ~$297.7 billion equity portfolio (at fair value) is too top-heavy, with 65% concentrated in only 5 companies (AAPL, AXP, BAC, KO, and CVX), although reduced Y/Y from 71%. In my opinion, the portfolio is suffering from dead equity drag. A major example is The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) stock. Abel pointed out in the 2026 letter, “Our return has been well short of adequate.” With the departure of Berkshire representatives from the KHC board and the shift to a one-quarter reporting lag, Berkshire is trapped in huge, underperforming legacy consumer staples. Because of antitrust scrutiny and Berkshire's size, Abel cannot easily acquire new elephants (the $9.5 billion OxyChem deal in January 2026 moves the needle by less than 1%). If Apple or American Express suffers a multi-year cyclical downturn, Berkshire has no mechanism (or a clear investment strategy at least), in my opinion, to shift/rotate its $300 billion equity portfolio easily (there are issues like blind followers who will magnify the exit impact on the stocks’ prices). This factor may be forcing Berkshire to accept sub-S&P 500 returns for the next decade.

Berkshire Hathaway Stock Valuation Analysis

On valuing Berkshire Hathaway now, my stance is that the standard use of a consolidated Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] and scenario-based Sum-of-the-Parts [SOTP] model is not logical. It fails to isolate the zero-cost leverage of the insurance float, double-counts/under-counts the embedded deferred tax liabilities in the equity portfolio, and confuses highly regulated capital-intensive utility cash flows with asset-light franchise cash flows. To value Berkshire Hathaway with logical precision under the new CEO Gregory E. Abel's administration, I use a bifurcated + hybrid SOTP method to separate Berkshire into two different systems. The first system is the Liquidity and Investment Portfolio (which is valued at adjusted mark-to-market). The second system is the operating economics (which I value through capitalized normalized earnings, and in that I am removing all investment income to avoid double counting).

For System-1, the Liquidity and Investment Portfolio (the Float-Funded fraction), to value the asset side of Berkshire, I am totaling the fair value of all cash and investments and then deducting the non-operating debt and the true economic cost of deferred taxes. First, I am doing gross asset aggregation (as of Dec. 31, 2025). I derived the total fair value of gross liquidity and investments at $708.9 billion based on [cash/equivalents + U.S. T-bills of $373.3 billion ($369 billion from Insurance/Other + $4.1 billion from BHE/BNSF)] plus [Equity Securities (Mark-to-Market) of $297.8 billion] plus [fixed maturity securities of $17.8 billion] plus [Equity method investments (KHC, OXY, Berkadia) of $20 billion; it reflects the $5 billion Q2 impairment on Kraft Heinz and $5.7 billion Q4 impairment on Occidental that is reducing carrying values to strict fair market level].

Then, I am doing the liability and tax friction deductions. For this, insurance and other debt are $(45.8) billion, but I do not deduct the $83.3 billion of BHE/BNSF debt here, as that is non-recourse and serviced by their own operational cash and will be integrated in the OpCo multiples. Then, for the Deferred Tax Liability [DTL] discount, I do not deduct the full $48.4 billion DTL on unrealized gains. To answer why? Ok, so Berkshire holds assets like Coca-Cola and American Express perpetually. Therefore, the DTL is an interest-free loan from the U.S. Treasury. I am assuming a conservative 20-year average timeframe for realization and a 7% discount rate (rounded up above Berkshire's 10-year median of 6.65% for a conservative floor). The calculations lead to a present value [PV] of this $48.4 billion tax liability at ~$12.5 billion (to deduct). For the insurance float (at $176 billion), I deduct nothing because Berkshire already, for the long term, on a constant basis, is deriving underwriting profit (that makes a negative cost of float). Then, this $176 billion liability (in my opinion) works as a revolving equity. Thus, the final fair value of System-1 is $650.6 billion based on [$708.9 billion–$45.8 billion–$12.5 billion (DTL's PV)].

Above all these core businesses, corporate overhead/unallocated led to a $(0.5) billion in net income. I apply BRK.B’s 5Y average of forward P/E (22.54x) to value this segment. That provides a fair value of -$11.27 [–$0.5 billion x 22.54x]. Therefore, the final fair value of System-2 is (total of OpCo) $487.4 billion [$273 billion + $104 billion + $55.7 billion + $66 billion – $11.27 billion]. With all that, Berkshire Hathaway’s total intrinsic value is $1.138 trillion [Net Investments and Liquidity’s $650.6 billion + Operating Businesses’ $487.4 billion]. That means the per-share fair/floor price (based on 2,157,335,139 Class B Equivalent Shares) is $527.5. At the current market price of $474.58, this means an upside of +11.15% (I consider it a margin of safety). In my opinion, the $527.5 fair price is a static measure as it does not integrate the Abel Arbitrage.

To clarify more on this, the arbitrage is in the forward mechanisms Greg Abel initiated in Q1-FY2026. The first mechanism is the resumption of buybacks, as in March 2026, Abel resumed the stock repurchase program. This move validates that Berkshire stock is trading below intrinsic value, in my stance, as every dollar of the $373.3 billion cash pile used to buy back stock at $474.58 accretes value to the remaining stock. If Abel deploys, say, only $25 billion in FY2026 at current prices, the fair value per share pushes toward $540+. The second mechanism is the Tokio Marine Yen-Advantage, as Abel is not letting the cash sit idle. By issuing ¥272.3 billion ($1.7 billion) in debt at 3.084% in April 2026 to buy Tokio Marine stock, Berkshire is locking in fixed, low-cost yen liabilities to acquire high-yielding compounding Japanese financial equity. This is a synthetic ROE multiplier that generic SOTP models do not consider.

To add more here, ~$373.3 billion cash is an embedded call option. The cash pile is currently yielding ~4.5%-5% in T-Bills (deriving ~$18 billion annually). However, what I see is that the true value of this cash is its optionality. In a systemic market dislocation, Berkshire can be the lender/buyer of last resort. The cash is worth way more than its face value because it backs Berkshire in taking advantage of the next macroeconomic crisis.

Berkshire Hathaway Stock Technical Analysis

The weekly price chart [5Y] of Berkshire Hathaway stock points to a large bull run from the $260s in 2022 to a double-top distribution phase near $540 in early 2025. Following this distribution, Berkshire stock entered a complex corrective phase. I use standard Fibonacci pivot points, and the stock broke below the pivot at $493 in Q1-FY2026. This move is shifting the mid-term bias from consolidation to bearish correction. This $493 level now serves as the main overhead resistance and the gateway to a renewed bull trend. Support 1 [S1] at $454.05 is the immediate support level and may find aggressive buying pressure considering a long lower wick on the current candlestick. Importantly, this technical S1 level is in line with the Abel Buyback Floor. At this level, Berkshire's stock repurchase program becomes highly accretive for forward value. For the worst case, S2 at $430 is the core macro tail-risk support and represents the change of polarity originating from 2024-2025. A break to this level is highly improbable without a big macroeconomic drop.

Beyond price structure, on trend analysis, the 5-week EMA is below the 13-week EMA, and the current price ($474.58) is below both. This technically confirms that BRK.B is in a short-term downtrend emerging within the consolidation. However, the distance between the current price and the 5-week EMA ($478.82) is thin (less than 1%). The price action over the last 3-4 months also points to a flattening trend, as the moving averages have stopped accelerating downward and are plateauing. A weekly close above the 13-week EMA ($485.52) and the Central Pivot ($493.01).

On the MACD, RSI, and Full Stochastic oscillators, I see a possible reversal. MACD (-6.695) is below the Signal Line (-5.532), and the Histogram is negative (-1.163). The MACD reflects the heavy distribution that occurred throughout Q1 2026. However, looking closely at the histogram trend, the negative expansion has maxed out. The bars are reversing and signaling that the downward energy of the trend is mostly depleted. Whereas, RSI is at 42.06. In continued bull trends, as a high-quality mega-cap, Berkshire Hathaway stock rarely hits the theoretical oversold level of 30 on a weekly timeframe. As of now, I consider the 40-42 range as the bear market bounce floor. The RSI has struck this exact low-40s level and flattened out back-to-back since mid-2022. Since then, it has refused to break lower despite negative news catalysts (like the Q4 earnings drop and wildfire litigation) that indicate strong institutional support, in my opinion.

Finally, Full Stochastic [%K (24.26) has crossed above %D (19.37) after emerging from below 20] is providing a strong buy signal (a leading indication). After plunging into an oversold state (sub-20) during the March/April 2026 selloff, the faster %K line has sharply hooked upward and crossed the slower %D line. All in all, I am observing a bearish MACD (lagging) but a bullish Stochastic crossover (leading) occurring near a major Fibonacci Support level (S1). All these flags point to a trend reversal possibility, meaning sellers of Berkshire stock may be out of ammunition. At this exact level, three forces are colliding to halt the price decline. The first one is Greg Abel's aggressive resumption of the buyback program that is acting as the ultimate bid in the market (Fundamental Force). The second force is Abel's personal $15.3 million open-market purchase, which is signaling internal confidence in intrinsic undervaluation (psychological force). Lastly, the third force is the Full Stochastic %K/%D bullish crossover originating from a deep oversold state (Algorithmic Force).

Alpha Protocol For Berkshire Hathaway Q1 2026 Earnings

The upcoming Q1-FY2026 earnings (early May) may happen along with the May 2, 2026, Annual Shareholder Meeting. There will be vital disclosures in Berkshire Hathaway’s history, as there will be the first unfiltered look at Gregory E. Abel’s autonomous capital allocation pattern as CEO. There are 4 monitorables to define what to do with Berkshire’s next earnings.

Monitorable 1: The 27-Day Buyback Extrapolation Trade (The Main Catalyst)

In the 2025 Annual Report, it was revealed that Berkshire has done zero stock repurchases in Q4-FY2025. However, on March 4, 2026, Abel resumed the buyback program, and that was followed by his personal $15.3 million stock purchase. For Q1-FY2026, this means the Q1 Cash Flow Statement will capture ~27 days of Abel’s buyback activity. This 27-day timeframe is the most vital point in the Q1 earnings, as it provides the algorithmic baseline to work out Abel's annualized capital return pace. On this side, an aggressive market-based buyback (say >0.09% of mCap in 27 days, annualized ~10%) may mark that Abel views the sub-$500 level as undervalued and is willing to aggressively use the $373.3 billion cash pile. Any aggressiveness on buybacks immediately sets a hard price floor at the S1 Fibonacci level. But at the same time, if buybacks are negligible despite the formal resumption announcement. This may indicate that Abel is on Buffett's extreme conservatism towards the intrinsic value. Wall Street may punish Berkshire stock for the dead equity drag. In that case, Smart Money may view the $373.3 billion cash pile as an asset that is losing purchasing power to inflation, and that can push Berkshire stock toward the S2 support of $430.

Monitorable 2: The Ring-Fencing of BHE Liabilities (The Hidden Alpha)

Wall Street is terrified that the Berkshire parent will have to bail out BHE. I will scrutinize the Commitments and Contingencies and Notes Payable sections of the Q1–10-Q for the legal phrasing on BHE’s debt. I will look for the reiteration of the specific phrase like “Berkshire does not guarantee any borrowings of BHE, BNSF, or their subsidiaries.” Further, I will check for Abel to state in the Q&A on May 2 that Berkshire will not inject parent capital to fund BHE's legal liabilities/supersedeas bonds. In my opinion, allowing BHE to counter severe distress/even ring-fencing it through Chapter 11 reorganization may prove that Abel is a rational allocator who will protect the $373.3 billion fortress balance sheet at the expense of a compromised utility subsidiary. If Abel confirms the regulatory compact is broken and cuts off BHE's allowance, the contagion risk to the parent company drops to zero. On the downside, any indication of a parent-level capital injection, like an intercompany loan to PacifiCorp/parent-backed surety bonds to cover the Oregon appeals, will indicate a large breach of Berkshire's decentralized corporate veil. If the $373.3 billion cash pile is opened to plaintiff attorneys, BRK.B may face a strong multi-billion-dollar valuation haircut.

Monitorable 3: The BNSF Margin Arbitrage Confirmation

In my opinion, Q1-FY2026 is the first clean quarter (post-2025 transition) to analyze the pure operational leverage of BNSF’s new labor flexibility. I will be looking for the operating ratio [OR]. To be specific, I will monitor BNSF’s Q1 OpEx against freight revenues. If the operating ratio continues to drop, it may confirm that the SMART-TD deal has lowered the long-term cost floor. For instance, an operating ratio level of 63.5% means ~$460 million in new permanent annual cash flow that justifies multiple expansion for the BNSF segment from 20x (matching Union Pacific’s level) and adds ~$5.5 billion to Berkshire's hybrid SOTP valuation.

Monitorable 4: The Tokio Marine Yen-Spread Expansion

Because the Tokio Marine deal occurred in April, it will not be in the Q1 financials (that ended March 31). But it will be listed in the Subsequent Events section. I will not focus on the Q1 earnings data based on the Japanese equity line item. However, I will listen mainly to Ajit Jain during the May 2nd Q&A session. The game here is beyond dividend arbitrage, as it is a reinsurance synergy. If Ajit Jain outlines a strategic alliance where National Indemnity [NICO] assumes large tranches of Tokio Marine's catastrophic risk (that is, leveraging Berkshire's $176 billion float in a market where Japanese insurers are seeking to offload risk), this transforms the Tokio Marine stake from a passive holding into its distribution channel for BHRG. If confirmed, I will adjust the intrinsic valuation of the Reinsurance segment upward.

All in all, BRK.B/BRK.A is a Strong Buy because Wall Street has penalized the stock for temporary Q4-FY2025 insurance cyclicality and legacy impairments and is mostly ignoring the certainty of Abel’s new capital return system, in my opinion. At 1.34x book value, the $373.3 billion liquidity backs a strong margin of safety and works as a coiled spring for EPS accretion. As Buffett’s era has ended, if Abel progressively cannibalizes the float and optimizes the industrial base, Berkshire may re-rate toward its $540+ fair value, which may uplift its status as a new haven (mainly for a risk-averse investor base).

data.title: Apple: Next CEO Should Rent Out Siri, Not Rebuild It
data.description:
I am revisiting my bull case on Apple Inc. (AAPL) after the announcement that John Ternus will become CEO on September 1, 2026, with Tim Cook moving to executive chairman.

In my view, this CEO transition comes at a moment when the market is still debating what Apple should be. The key question that John Ternus has to address is whether Apple is an AI platform, a hardware innovator, or a cash compounder built on distribution and ecosystem control.

My core view remains constructive, although it's conditional on one factor. Apple must stay disciplined on AI. By disciplined, I mean reducing as much as possible any cash burn (some call it investment) in any in-house LLM models or in the new upgraded Siri.

As proven by Q1 results and Q2 guidance, consumers aren't that concerned with AI features as investors are. Therefore, the fact that the company is behind the Mag 7 in the AI race is actually a positive. Why?

Simply because Apple owns the distribution channel, particularly in the U.S. In my view, charging LLM providers to solve Siri queries makes significantly more sense than building data centers and developing their own LLM model to do the same thing.

I'll be clear. If the new CEO pivots toward a costly and lower return effort to compete directly on foundation models, the bull case becomes materially less attractive. Until I see signs of this pivot, I remain optimistic about the company, and I reiterate my strong buy rating.

Tim Cook Stepping Down as CEO

The main headline this week is the announcement that John Ternus will become CEO on September 1, 2026, while Tim Cook becomes executive chairman.

Ternus is 50, has a 25 year history inside Apple, and is widely recognized as a product focused engineer. According to Apple, he joined the product design team in 2001, became a hardware engineering vice president in 2013, and joined the executive team in 2021.

So, he’s a relatively new executive and product perfectionist, which is both a plus and a minus.

On the positive side, Apple has been perceived by the market as a laggard in the AI race. You may remember the March 2025 announcement that these Siri upgrades (i.e., the new Siri) were delayed to 2026.

Well, we're well into 2026, and the old Siri is still there. Now, does that really matter? It turns out the answer is no, as I discussed in a previous coverage. Here is an excerpt from the last earnings call that proves that end users don't care as much about AI as investors do:

And based on that, we're in a supply chase mode to meet the very high levels of customer demand. We are currently constrained. And at this point, it's difficult to predict when supply and demand will balance.

On the negative side, as a product perfectionist, I am afraid he may double down on the AI strategy, or even worse, on the Apple Vision Pro.

I have to say that I'm not encouraged by Apple’s $3,499 Vision Pro headset, especially when Meta’s Ray Ban smart glasses had become a surprise hit at a fraction of the Vision Pro's price. That's why the wearables segment growth is pretty much nonexistent.

As for the AI strategy, I argued many times that Apple's edge is distribution, not intelligence. That's why I was so encouraged by the Gemini partnership earlier this year. I certainly prefer Siri to run on Gemini (or route prompts to this LLM) rather than Apple creating its own LLM model.

In fact, headlines like the one below are particularly encouraging.

Why?

In my view, the more AI providers like Gemini or Claude compete for placement, the higher the bidding price to be the default LLM on Siri. Similar to search. To be clear, Apple hasn't announced a paid default-placement auction for Siri yet. In my view, this is a potential catalyst worth monitoring.

Therefore, I would keep a very close eye on the new CEO's remarks in September, as a U-turn toward Apple Intelligence (run by an in-house LLM model) would be detrimental to the stock, in my view.

Why build your own model and burn tokens when you can charge others to be the default LLM provider? That’s why I like Tim’s current strategy.

More Leadership Changes

Aside from the main CEO headline, Arthur Levinson will shift from non-executive chairman to lead independent director, and Ternus will join the board.

A quick look at the latest 14A shows the stock holdings among the top beneficial owners. Tim Cook is the 4th largest owner of shares, and Arthur Levinson is the 3rd.

Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised to see a wave of insider selling activity prior to the transition.

Speaking of insider activity, it would be very encouraging to see John Ternus opening longs on the company after (or even before) he takes over Tim in September.

The transition plan also includes promoting Johny Srouji to chief hardware officer, effective immediately, filling the most obvious hole created by Ternus’s promotion.

There are a lot of moving parts, so I will keep a close eye on the next earnings call for any hints that suggest a U-turn on the AI distribution model of the company. Speaking of earnings, here is what I'm monitoring heading into the Q2 2026 earnings release.

Q2 Preview

Apple guided Q2 2026 revenue growth to 13% to 16%. The consensus was looking for only 10%, so management's guidance prompted upward revisions to the Street's revenue models:

The stock price is still lagging behind the 12-month revenue forecast trend (blue line below), so that's a good sign.

What I want to see in the next earnings call is continued momentum in iPhone and Services, which reached new all-time highs back in Q1 2026. Interestingly, back in Q1, Apple led global smartphone shipments and grew 5% yoy even as overall industry shipments fell because of memory shortages.

Therefore, I think analysts will double down during the Q&A on how higher memory prices affect demand and the company’s bottom line. I say double down, because in the last call, one of the analysts asked the following:

Maybe to start with -- there's a lot of focus on the impact of memory to a host of companies, and I'd love to kind of get your perspective when you folks are guiding gross margins up into the March quarter. Just talk about, A, your comfort in securing the bits that you need for shipments? And B, how do we think about memory inflation flowing through Apple's model over time?

Here is Tim's reply:

From a memory point of view, to answer your question, memory had a minimal impact on the Q1, so the December quarter gross margin. We do expect it to be a bit more of an impact to the Q2 gross margin and that was comprehended in the outlook of 48% to 49% that Kevan gave earlier.

Beyond Q2, we don't obviously provide outlooks beyond the current quarter. But we do continue to see market pricing for memory increasing significantly. As always, we'll look at a range of options to deal with that. So hopefully, that gives you the full view.

I’m not sure about you, but that still didn’t clear up the main question investors had: whether Apple would raise prices or absorb the cost pressure as memory pricing goes up.

For reference, I include below TrendForce's latest forecast for memory prices in Q2 2026 :

Overall, if I see a slow Q3 FY26 gross margin guidance, that could be an indication that the company is absorbing costs. On the contrary, if I see a slow Q3FY26 revenue growth guidance, that may suggest that they are passing the costs to consumers.

For reference, the Street is looking for 8.75% yoy growth in Q3 FY26. In fact, the trend below is not encouraging:

Valuation

Despite not being in the AI race, the stock is trading at a relatively rich premium. Investors are paying today 32x for next year's earnings. In comparison, I include a table showing the Magnificent 7 group:

Apple isn’t cheap, although, in my view, 32x forward earnings is not in overvalued territory. I also think there’s room for multiple expansion if the company surprises the Street’s top-line models, as it did in Q1 FY26.

Looking Ahead

Heading into the September CEO transition, I would keep a very close eye on Ternus’s messaging on AI.

As I noted in this piece, my bull case holds as long as Apple keeps treating AI as a distribution layer and monetizes default placement on Siri, as the Gemini partnership suggests.

I would get really spooked if the new leadership takes a U-turn and follows the steps of Meta in its AI strategy.

In the upcoming earnings release, I’ll closely monitor how much memory inflation shows up in Q3 FY26 guidance, both in revenue and gross margin, after Q2’s 13% to 16% revenue outlook and 48% to 49% gross margin guidance. Even if management doesn’t disclose whether it plans price hikes or will absorb the jump in memory costs, the guidance will likely be a telltale sign.

Overall, I remain bullish on Apple. My conviction didn't change much, however, I sold my position earlier this year, as I've been raising cash during the Hormuz selloff. A few weeks back, I decided to bet on the AI bottlenecks, as I believe there is more juice to be squeezed (i.e., higher returns).

data.title: Ban On Corporate Home Buying? No Sweat For American Homes 4 Rent
data.description:
There are very few bipartisan issues when it comes to our national government.

Priorities can be diverse, and the focus of each political party, or even each political candidate, can heavily vary based on the electorate that they're trying to appeal to. One aspect of everyday life that we can all agree on is that over the last decade, the cost of goods has risen heavily.

Typically, inflation is a quiet force that slowly erodes the value of each dollar you have in your savings account. When it moves at the desired 2%-3% rate, you don't really notice it from day to day or even from year to year. It's the slow drip that erodes value, yet if you think about it over multiple decades, the cost of things has risen quite a lot.

It wasn't that long ago that there were folks complaining to me about how gas used to be able to be purchased for 50 cents a gallon. It was seen as unimaginable that gasoline would break above the $2 a gallon point or even the $3 a gallon point. You're getting the same amount of gasoline, but the value of your dollar buying it becomes less and less. This is the impact of inflation.

A major catalyst for inflation in the last decade was the massive government bailouts and spending by governments around the globe to restart their economies after COVID-19. Instead of it taking three decades for inflation to become heavily impactful, we experienced a massive spike in inflation in 10 years. Even though inflation may be slowing down as a whole, it is a cumulative effect. Inflation, when measured, is the rate at which goods are getting more expensive, or your dollar is getting less valuable. That means when inflation slows, all of that previously built-up devaluation is still there.

This leads us back to what has become a bipartisan issue within our national government, and that is the focus on. Affordability. How to achieve affordability may not be agreed upon, but the need for affordability in the average American life has become a forefront issue. One aspect of the American dream that is getting a lot of attention regarding affordability is housing costs. The cost to buy a house has risen dramatically because, typically, home values climb in line with inflation. So with the rapid increase in inflation since COVID-19, home values have rapidly climbed as well, but individuals earning power has not. Your annual paycheck increase is meant to help you keep pace with inflation, but when inflation was spiking to high levels, it was not offsetting that effectively. Therefore, the prices of homes have climbed with inflation and faster than real wage-earning power.

So what is the solution? For some, they believe that it's not inflation's fault (the government's fault) that home values have climbed so rapidly, but that it is big corporations' fault. They believe that if corporations stopped buying homes, there would be a sudden decline in demand for them, and their values would fall quickly. This is a double-edged sword when taken at face value. You might be able to achieve a lower price for those individuals who haven't purchased a home yet and are willing to do so. You also risk hurting all of those who are using their home's equity as a retirement tool or a line of credit. If all of their homes suddenly get devalued, you're now hurting all of those individuals as well. You're helping some to hurt by hurting others.

Interestingly, homes owned by corporations are not a massive part of the overall housing market. Brookings determined that large institutional investors own only 3% of the single-family rental market. This reality hasn't stopped all levels of the American government from considering measures to try to increase affordability by reducing market activity by corporations or large institutional investors. You're considered a large institutional investor if you own 100 or more homes. The government loves to blame someone else for issues that they've caused, and corporations are an easy target.

So how can you and I benefit from this wide-scale misunderstanding and the likelihood that there really will not be anything effectively done by the government to solve the problem?

Today, I want to examine an opportunity that has sold off due to investors' fears of potential governmental action.

A Solution: Build, Don't Buy

One solution to avoiding any issues of institutional owners buying more homes is to not buy homes but to build them. Today's focus will be on American Homes 4 Rent (AMH). AMH is a single-family rental-focused real estate investment trust (REIT).

In January, President Trump signed an executive order aimed at reducing institutional investors' perceived bidding up of the housing market. He additionally signed further executive orders attempting to make housing more affordable in March. Overwhelmingly, however, the impact of these executive orders has been muted on the housing market, as home values continue to be strong even in the face of higher interest rates slowing down house-buying activity.

AMH was founded in 2012 and has focused on owning and renting single-family dwellings. Furthermore, they've moved from being a purchaser of properties to a developer of them, meaning that overwhelmingly they are now building homes that they then turn around and rent out or building communities that they rent out the homes within.

Moreover, AMH regularly sells homes into the housing market, meaning that they are now a net provider of homes, not a purchaser and consolidator of home ownership.

This allows us to sidestep many of the issues highlighted by the government when it comes to institutional ownership of homes. Even if they were to ban the ability for institutions or large institutions to purchase homes, it would not prevent them from building them. It would be silly to think that the government would actively prevent homes from being built while trying to increase housing affordability and overall housing stock.

AMH is structured as an REIT, meaning that the vast majority of its revenues must be doled out to shareholders as dividends. They can only retain about 10% of their taxable revenue at most to maintain their REIT status, and therefore, shares must be issued, or debt must be used to continue growth. Another option is capital recycling. This is one way AMH effectively funds its ability to continue to reward shareholders and grow. They will build homes, which they then turn around and sell at a profit, distributing what they must to shareholders and recycling the capital into additional home development. As long as home prices remain elevated and home demand remains strong, they're able to do this quite profitably.

Financial Check Up

AMH has a wide footprint across the United States. This allows them to adapt to the varied economic conditions of each region.
